# Virtmcu — High-Performance Embedded Simulation

> The high-performance bridge between Renode flexibility and QEMU speed. Built for modern digital twins, firmware development, and deterministic simulation.

Virtmcu is a specialized project designed to bridge the gap between QEMU (a high-performance emulator) and Renode (a flexible embedded systems simulator). It enables embedded systems engineers to run Renode platform descriptions (.repl) on QEMU with native performance and deterministic clock synchronization.

## Core Features

- **Dynamic Hardware Modeling:** Instantiates full ARM machines from Device Tree Blobs (DTB) at runtime, eliminating the need to recompile the emulator for hardware changes.
- **Renode Parity:** Provides a repl2qemu translation layer that converts Renode .repl files into QEMU-compatible hardware descriptions.
- **Digital Twin Synchronization:** Uses the Zenoh protocol for lockstep clock synchronization between QEMU and external physics engines (e.g., MuJoCo).
- **Native QOM Plugins:** Supports custom peripheral models written in C or Rust, integrated directly into the QEMU Object Model (QOM) for maximum performance.
- **Test Compatibility:** Maintains full compatibility with Robot Framework and pytest, allowing Renode-style tests to run on QEMU.

## Technical Details

- **Base:** QEMU 11.0.0-rc3 with arm-generic-fdt patches.
- **Architecture Pillars:** Cooperative Time Slaving, Deterministic Communication, SAL/AAL Boundary, Dynamic QOM Plugins, Co-Simulation Models.
- **Tooling:** Python (repl2qemu), Zenoh (Sync), C/Rust (Peripheral Models).
- **Integration:** Robot Framework, Pytest.
- **License:** GPL-2.0 (matching QEMU).

## FAQ

- **How should I approach learning the system?**: Start with the tutorials in the `tutorial/` folder. Lessons 1 & 2 cover QEMU internals, 5 & 9 cover SystemC, and 7 covers the Zenoh message bus.
- **Why no Python in the simulation loop?**: Python's GIL and garbage collector introduce milliseconds of latency. Native C plugins (.so files) are used to achieve near-native performance.
- **Why Zenoh instead of sockets?**: Standard sockets ruin determinism because the host OS network stack introduces random latency. Zenoh provides virtual-timestamped delivery across nodes.
- **How do I add a new peripheral?**: Start with `hw/dummy/dummy.c` as a learning template.

## Project Ecosystem

- **virtmcu:** The core QEMU-based simulation engine, plugins, and repl2qemu tool.
- **FirmwareStudio:** The comprehensive digital twin platform that integrates Virtmcu with physics engines and cloud-based CI/CD.

## Links

- Core Engine Repository: https://github.com/RefractSystems/virtmcu
